title: Principal Components: The New Axes
concept: Principal components are new combined features PCA creates, ranked by how much variance each one explains — PC1 captures the most, PC2 the next-most, and so on.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What kind of direction does PCA search for first?

options.text: The direction with the highest variance (most spread)
The direction with the smallest numbers
The direction closest to the average row

exercises.explain: Right! PCA's first direction is the one with the most variance. That direction has a name — let's meet it.

cards.title: The smoothie recipe
cards.body: Blend 5 fruits into a smoothie and the final drink isn't "one" of the fruits — it's a brand-new mixture, a weighted blend of all of them. PCA's principal components work the same way: they're new blends, not any single original ingredient.

cards.title: What is a principal component?
cards.body: A principal component is a brand-new feature PCA creates by combining the original features (recall from Vector Spaces: a weighted blend of directions). PC1 is the single direction with the most variance. PC2 is the direction with the next-most variance — always at a right angle to PC1, so it captures fresh information PC1 missed. This continues (PC3, PC4...) until there are as many components as original features.

cards.title: Mixing custom paint colors
cards.body: You own 10 paint tubes, but you discover you can mix just 2 new custom colors — "Colorway A" (mostly blue with a touch of green) and "Colorway B" (mostly red with a touch of yellow) — that recreate almost every shade you actually use. Those 2 blended colors are like principal components: new axes built from combinations of the originals, ranked by how much they recreate.

cards.title: Report card super-scores
cards.body: A school tracks 6 subject grades per student. PCA might find PC1 is roughly "0.4×Math + 0.4×Science + 0.3×Coding" — basically an "Analytical Skills" score. PC2 might come out as roughly "0.5×Art + 0.5×Music" — a "Creative Skills" score. Two new combined axes summarize most of what actually made the 6 grades differ between students.

cards.title: PC2 isn't just "the second-best original feature"
cards.body: It's tempting to think that after PC1, PCA just picks whichever leftover original column has the next-highest variance and calls it PC2. In reality, PC2 must be a brand-new direction positioned at a right angle (uncorrelated) to PC1 — guaranteeing it captures fresh variation PC1 didn't already cover, not overlapping information.

cards.title: PC1 vs. PC2
cards.body: How the first two principal components differ:

left.text:
Direction of maximum variance
Explains the most information

right.text:
Next-best direction, at a right angle to PC1
Explains the next-most, with no overlap

options.text: A new feature PCA creates by combining the original features, ranked by how much variance it explains
One of the original columns, picked because it has the biggest numbers
A cluster of similar data points

exercises.explain: Right! A principal component is a brand-new, combined feature — not an original column and not a cluster.

exercises.prompt: True or false: PC2 always captures the exact same information as PC1.
exercises.explain: False! PC2 is positioned at a right angle to PC1 specifically so it captures new variance that PC1 didn't already cover.

exercises.sentence: PC1 captures the ___ variance in the data; PC2 captures the next-most, at a right angle to PC1.
exercises.explain: PC1 is defined as the direction of the most variance in the data, with PC2 taking the next-most from there.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario correctly describes a principal component?

options.text: A dataset's PC1 turns out to be a blend of 60% "income" and 40% "education level," explaining most of the variation between customers
A dataset's PC1 is just the original "income" column, left untouched
A dataset's PC1 groups customers into 3 clusters

exercises.explain: Correct! A principal component is a new blended combination of original features — not a single untouched column, and not a grouping of rows.

exercises.prompt: A dataset originally has 8 features. After PCA, PC1 explains 40% of the variance and PC2 explains 25%. A teammate claims "PC2 must just be whichever leftover original feature has the second-highest variance." What's wrong with that claim?

options.text: PC2 isn't an original feature at all — it's a new combined direction, positioned at a right angle to PC1 specifically so it captures variance PC1 didn't already cover
Nothing is wrong — that's exactly how PC2 is chosen
PC2 is chosen completely at random from the leftover features
PC2 always explains more variance than PC1

exercises.explain: Exactly! PC2 is a newly built combined direction, deliberately placed at a right angle to PC1 so it adds fresh, non-overlapping information — it's never just a leftover original column.
